title: Frame loom geometric tapestry
subtitle: Weaving chevrons, stepped diagonals, and triangles on a frame loom using interlocking and dovetail joins
excerpt: Geometric shapes in tapestry weaving are built row by row: moving the colour boundary by one warp thread each row steps a diagonal, holding it in place builds a vertical boundary. This 25 x 35 cm piece uses four weft colours to produce stepped chevrons, filled triangles, and horizontal bands. It introduces the interlocking weft and dovetail joins used to manage clean colour boundaries without leaving slits in the cloth.
sourceNotes: Geometric tapestry weaving using interlocking and dovetailing joins is documented throughout contemporary and traditional tapestry practice. Method is observation-led and consistent with North American tapestry guild instruction.

content.text: Tapestry colour boundaries are managed with joins.
produces a softer alternating edge. For diagonal lines and steps, neither join is needed: the colour boundary simply moves left or right by one warp thread on each row.

items.name: Warp yarn (smooth cotton at DK weight)
Weft yarn in 4 colours (wool at DK-aran weight)

content.text: Step 1: Plan the design on graph paper

Draw the design on graph paper at 1 square per warp thread and 1 square per pick. Mark each colour area and count the rows needed for each shape. Assign colours: for example, a cream background, a terracotta chevron band, a dark teal triangle, and a rust base stripe. Count the weft yardage needed per colour area before winding bobbins.
Step 2: Warp the loom and weave the base

Warp the frame loom at 4-5 ends per cm to a 25 cm weaving width. Weave a 3 cm header in the warp yarn to spread the ends evenly. Then weave the base stripe in the first colour across the full width for the planned number of picks.

To build the chevron, work two colour areas simultaneously from the centre of the warp outward. On each pick, move the colour boundary one warp thread further from centre (left for the left half, right for the right half). This steps the diagonal outward row by row, forming a V. At the boundary between the two halves, use the interlocking join: bring the left-colour weft to the boundary, loop it around the right-colour weft between two adjacent warp threads, then return it. The loop sits between the warps, invisible from the front.
Step 4: Fill triangles with dovetail joins

For the triangle areas against the background colour, use dovetailing at the diagonal boundary: alternate which colour wraps the boundary warp thread on each pick. One pick, the triangle colour wraps the boundary warp and returns; the next pick, the background colour wraps it and returns. This produces a soft alternating edge rather than a hard seam or a slit. Keep both colours woven simultaneously across each row to maintain even tension.

items.fix: Use the interlocking join for any straight vertical boundary. For a finished piece, unsewn slits must be stitched closed on the reverse after removing from the loom.
items.cause: Both wefts turned around their own warp threads without joining
items.problem: A slit forms at the vertical colour boundary

items.fix: Move the boundary by exactly one thread per row. Mark each boundary position on the design diagram and tick it off as you complete each row.
items.cause: The colour boundary moved by inconsistent numbers of threads on consecutive rows
items.problem: The diagonal line is jagged rather than a smooth step

items.fix: Beat both colour areas with the same pressure. Check that the weft arc is the same size for both colours before beating.
items.cause: One colour area woven with tighter tension than the adjacent area
items.problem: The tapestry draws in at colour-change rows
